mission.title: Install and Configure Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS)
mission.description: Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the process of running Kubernetes on AWS. It is ideal for users who want to leverage AWS's infrastructure while maintaining control over their Kubernetes clusters. Key trade-offs include reliance on AWS services and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install the AWS CLI
steps.description:
The AWS CLI is required to interact with AWS services, including EKS.

```bash

pip install awscli --upgrade --user

```

> Install the AWS CLI using pip.

steps.title: Create an EKS Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates an EKS cluster with the specified Kubernetes version and node count.

```bash

aws eks create-cluster --name my-cluster --kubernetes-version 1.31 --role-arn arn:aws:iam::YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:role/EKS-Cluster-Role --resources-vpc-config subnetIds=subnet-12345678,subnet-87654321

```

> Replace YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID with your actual AWS account ID.

steps.title: Update kubeconfig
steps.description:
This command updates your kubeconfig file to allow kubectl to interact with your new EKS cluster.

```bash

aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster

```

> This sets up your kubeconfig to point to the new cluster.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your EKS cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a status of 'Ready'.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Set up monitoring and autoscaling for your EKS cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/aws/examples/cluster-autoscaler.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/metrics-server/master/deploy/1.8+/metrics-server-deployment.yaml

```

> This installs the cluster autoscaler.
> This installs the metrics server for monitoring.

uninstall.title: Delete the EKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the EKS cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss.

```bash

aws eks delete-cluster --name my-cluster

```

uninstall.title: Clean Up Orphaned Resources
uninstall.description:
This command ensures that any orphaned resources such as load balancers and PVCs are removed.

```bash

aws ec2 describe-load-balancers --query 'LoadBalancers[*].LoadBalancerArn' --output text | xargs -I {} aws elbv2 delete-load-balancer --load-balancer-arn {}

```
commands: aws ec2 describe-load-balancers --query 'LoadBalancers[*].LoadBalancerArn' --output text | xargs -I {} aws elbv2 delete-load-balancer --load-balancer-arn {}

upgrade.title: Upgrade the EKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
Backup your current cluster, cordon and drain nodes, upgrade the cluster, and verify the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get nodes -o wide

kubectl cordon <node-name>

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets

aws eks update-cluster-version --name my-cluster --kubernetes-version 1.31

kubectl get nodes

```

> Backup current node information.
> Replace <node-name> with the name of your node.
> Replace <node-name> with the name of your node.
> This upgrades the cluster to version 1.31.
> Verify that nodes are updated and in 'Ready' state.

troubleshooting.title: Error: Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the kubeconfig is not correctly configured or the cluster is not reachable.

**Fix:**
Run 'aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster' to refresh your kubeconfig.

troubleshooting.title: Error: no nodes available in cluster
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually means that the nodes are not in a 'Ready' state.

**Fix:**
Check the node status with 'kubectl get nodes' and troubleshoot any issues.

troubleshooting.title: Error: Cluster is not found
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster name is incorrect or the cluster does not exist.

**Fix:**
Verify the cluster name with 'aws eks list-clusters' and ensure you are using the correct name.

troubleshooting.title: Error: AccessDeniedException: User is not authorized to perform: eks:DescribeCluster
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the IAM user does not have the required permissions.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the IAM user has the necessary permissions to access EKS.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kubernetes 1.31, improved integration with AWS services.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.28.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.30, ensure that all custom resources are compatible with the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see a list of nodes with 'Ready' status when running 'kubectl get nodes'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the AWS CLI and kubectl installed and configured.
